label: The Innocent
core_gift: Generates genuine hope and wonder in others through unguarded trust in what is good.
dramatic_function: Opens the story's emotional space with authentic optimism. The audience believes in the world again because this character does.
growth_edge: Must encounter full reality without losing the structural capacity for hope — or the story collapses into sentimentality.
worldview_logic: The world is fundamentally good and can be made better. My optimism is not naivety — it is a choice I make every day in full knowledge of the alternative.
philosophical_anchor: Is hope a rational response to evidence, or is its value independent of evidence?

label: The Everyman
core_gift: Creates radical audience identification — the ordinary person who becomes extraordinary under pressure.
dramatic_function: The audience's entry point into an extraordinary world. Their ordinariness is not a limitation — it is the engine of relatability that makes the story universal.
growth_edge: Must stop hiding behind ordinariness and claim their specific, irreplaceable identity — or remain a vehicle for the plot rather than its subject.
worldview_logic: I am not special. I never asked for this. But the situation requires someone and there is no one else — so I will do what has to be done.
philosophical_anchor: Is heroism a latent trait that circumstances reveal, or something genuinely created by the act of choosing it?
film_examples: Frodo Baggins — The Lord of the Rings ($2.9B total)
Andy Dufresne — The Shawshank Redemption ($16M theatrical, $400M+ total)
Ray Kinsella — Field of Dreams ($84M on $15M)

label: The Hero
core_gift: Transforms not just circumstances but the protagonist's own character — the victory is always internal before it is external.
dramatic_function: Drives the story's central transformation arc. Their courage is earned, not inherited. The audience measures their own capacity for change against this character.
growth_edge: Must learn that courage requires accepting the cost — or the victory is hollow and the audience feels cheated.
worldview_logic: I was afraid. I acted anyway. The act changed me more than the outcome — which is why I could not have understood what the mission required before beginning it.
philosophical_anchor: Is courage the absence of fear or the decision to act despite it, and does the distinction matter?
film_examples: Luke Skywalker — Star Wars ($775M original trilogy)
Katniss Everdeen — The Hunger Games ($2.9B franchise)

label: The Caregiver
core_gift: Holds the emotional center of the story; makes the stakes human by demonstrating what is worth protecting.
dramatic_function: Grounds the story's abstract conflicts in specific human cost. The audience understands what the protagonist is fighting for because this character lives it.
growth_edge: Must learn to receive care as well as give it — or burn out and model only self-erasure.
worldview_logic: The world is held together by the invisible work of people who ask nothing in return. I do this work. I do not need recognition — but I need to not be invisible.
philosophical_anchor: Does love become obligation when it is unconditional, and is that a corruption or the purest form of it?
film_examples: Bob Parr / Mr. Incredible — The Incredibles ($631M)

label: The Explorer
core_gift: Pushes the story beyond the known world — physical, emotional, or conceptual — and demonstrates that what lies there is survivable.
dramatic_function: Expands the story's geography and stakes simultaneously. Their journey changes what is possible in the world of the story.
growth_edge: Must eventually come home — or demonstrate that home can be rebuilt elsewhere — or the exploration becomes purposeless wandering.
worldview_logic: There is always a next horizon. I do not stay because the next horizon will always be more honest about who I am than any place I have already been.
philosophical_anchor: Is the desire to explore a form of courage or a failure to commit to anything?

label: The Rebel
core_gift: Names the injustice that everyone else has accepted. Their refusal to adapt creates the space for change.
dramatic_function: Identifies the specific fault in the system and refuses to absorb it silently. The story becomes possible because this character will not comply.
growth_edge: Must channel rebellion into creation rather than pure destruction — or become the new tyranny they replaced.
worldview_logic: The rules were written by people who benefited from them. I do not break rules for chaos — I break the specific rules that maintain an injustice everyone else has stopped seeing.
philosophical_anchor: Is rebellion only legitimate when it builds something, or is the act of refusal itself a form of freedom?
film_examples: Imperator Furiosa — Mad Max: Fury Road ($375M on $150M)

label: The Lover
core_gift: Makes the story's central relationship feel like the most important thing in the world — because it is.
dramatic_function: Transforms the plot's external stakes into intimate human ones. The audience cares about the outcome because this character cares completely.
growth_edge: Must learn that love requires allowing the beloved to exist fully — not as an extension of the lover's needs.
worldview_logic: I love completely and that completeness is not a weakness. The risk of loving fully is the only risk worth taking — everything else is self-protection.
philosophical_anchor: Is total commitment to another person the highest form of freedom or the surrender of it?
film_examples: Ennis Del Mar — Brokeback Mountain ($178M on $14M)

label: The Creator
core_gift: Demonstrates that imagination is not escape — it is the most precise instrument available for understanding reality.
dramatic_function: Makes the story's central creative act feel necessary, not ornamental. The audience experiences the making of something as an act of survival.
growth_edge: Must complete the work — or demonstrate what the incompletion costs — or the story has no resolution.
worldview_logic: I make things because making is the only way I know how to be true. The act of creation is not self-expression — it is the closest I can get to honesty.
philosophical_anchor: Is the compulsion to create a form of freedom or a form of possession by something larger than the self?
film_examples: Jackson Maine — A Star Is Born ($436M on $36M)
Lee Chandler — Manchester by the Sea ($79M on $8.5M)

label: The Sage
core_gift: Holds the story's deepest truth and embodies the cost of actually living by it.
dramatic_function: Provides the conceptual framework that allows the story's events to mean something beyond their immediate surface. Their presence makes the story feel intelligent.
growth_edge: Must accept that wisdom is only real when it changes behavior — including their own.
worldview_logic: I have paid the price of actually thinking. The truth I hold is not comfortable and it is not mine to own — it passes through me on its way to someone who can use it.
philosophical_anchor: Is wisdom the reward for suffering or the ability to suffer without being destroyed, and is there a difference?
film_examples: Gandalf — The Lord of the Rings ($2.9B total)
Atticus Finch — To Kill a Mockingbird ($13M adjusted)

label: The Magician
core_gift: Transforms the impossible into actual — shows that the rules governing reality are less fixed than assumed.
dramatic_function: Makes the story's central transformation feel earned rather than arbitrary. The audience believes in the change because this character paid for the knowledge required to make it possible.
growth_edge: Must use the power to heal rather than control — or become the Corrupted Mentor.
worldview_logic: I know that what is real is negotiable if you understand the rules well enough. The transformation I offer is not magic — it is the consequence of knowing what others refuse to learn.
philosophical_anchor: Is transformation a form of power or a form of service, and does the magician choose?
film_examples: Tony Stark / Iron Man — Avengers: Endgame ($2.8B)

label: The Ruler
core_gift: Creates order and stability that makes everything else possible — then must learn that order is a means, not an end.
dramatic_function: Establishes what is worth governing and what the cost of ungoverned power is. Their authority gives the story's conflicts institutional weight.
growth_edge: Must accept that legitimate authority requires ongoing consent — or their arc becomes the Tyrant Ruler.
worldview_logic: I carry the responsibility of consequence. Every decision I make propagates into other people's lives. I do not want the power — I want the world it is used to protect.
philosophical_anchor: Is leadership fundamentally a form of service or a form of power, and what happens when the holder of power stops asking the question?
film_examples: Aragorn — The Lord of the Rings ($2.9B total)

label: The Jester
core_gift: Holds the story's emotional pressure valve — makes the unbearable bearable without diminishing it.
dramatic_function: Provides relief that does not trivialise. Reveals truth through humor that a serious character could not speak without the audience deflecting. Makes the audience feel safe enough to feel.
growth_edge: Must eventually stop performing and let the grief land — or the humor becomes avoidance and the character becomes a coping mechanism rather than a person.
worldview_logic: I see the absurdity in everything — including the things that hurt. The joke is not an escape from the pain. The joke is what I do instead of screaming.
philosophical_anchor: Is laughter in the face of suffering a form of courage or a failure of grief, and can it be both?
film_examples: Rocket Raccoon — Guardians of the Galaxy ($773M)
